title: GS Engineering & Construction Joins Global 'Earth Hour' Lights-Off Campaign - 서울경제신문
content: "We Will Actively Pursue Energy Conservation and Carbon Emission Reduction" GS Engineering & Construction (006360.KS) is participating in Earth Hour 2026, the world's largest lights-off campaign for the environment. According to GS Engineering & Construction on Saturday, Earth Hour is the world's largest climate crisis response campaign organized by the World Wide Fund for Nature (WWF), a nonprofit conservation organization. Since 2007, the campaign has been held every year on the last Saturday of March, with participants turning off lights for one hour starting at 8:30 p.m. to raise awareness of the severity of climate change and contribute to energy conservation and carbon emission reduction. For this year's 20th edition of the event, GS Engineering & Construction's headquarters building Gran Seoul in Jongno-gu, Seoul, and the GS E&C Seocho Tower in Seocho-dong, which houses its R&D center, turned off their lights for one hour starting at 8:30 p.m. on Saturday. Subsidiaries including Xi S&D, Xi C&A, Elysian Gangchon and Elysian Jeju also participated. In addition, GS Group affiliates including GS Tower, GS Neotek Building, Grand InterContinental Seoul Parnas, The Westin Josun Seoul Parnas and Nine Tree by Parnas locations joined the campaign. "Through our participation in Earth Hour, we will actively engage in eco-friendly campaigns such as climate change policy, energy conservation and carbon emission reduction," a GS Engineering & Construction official said. "In this ESG era, we will strive to lead the industry in the environmental sector as well." Meanwhile, GS Engineering & Construction received an overall grade of 'A (Excellent)' in the 2025 ESG ratings for listed companies released by the Korea Institute of Corporate Governance and Sustainability (KCGS) last year, earning recognition as a leading eco-friendly construction firm. The company has maintained an overall A rating for seven consecutive years since 2019. AI-translated from Korean. title: Goldman Sachs Group Inc Stock (GS) Moved Down by 3.86% on Mar 27: A Full Analysis - TradingKey
content: Goldman Sachs Group Inc (GS) moved down by 3.86%. The Banking & Investment Services sector is down by 1.31%. The company underperformed the industry. Top 3 stocks by turnover in the sector: Citigroup Inc (C) down 3.29%; JPMorgan Chase & Co (JPM) down 3.41%; Goldman Sachs Group Inc (GS) down 3.86%. Goldman Sachs (GS) experienced a notable decline in its share price today amid a broad market selloff influenced by mounting macroeconomic and geopolitical concerns. The broader United States stock market opened with a significant downturn, with investor sentiment notably dampened by developments stemming from the ongoing Middle East conflict. This geopolitical instability is fueling increased inflation expectations, particularly due to oil supply shocks, and is leading to anticipations of prolonged higher interest rates as the Federal Reserve is expected to delay rate cuts until clear progress on inflation is achieved. The firm itself recently raised its forecast for a U.S. recession probability, directly attributing this to the Middle East war's impact on oil supply and the subsequent higher inflation outlook. This internal assessment likely contributed to investor apprehension. Furthermore, recent data indicated a substantial drop in U.S. consumer sentiment during March, reaching its lowest point in several months, with a pronounced negative impact on higher-income consumers. Such a decline in consumer confidence can signal reduced economic activity, which poses headwinds for financial institutions heavily involved in investment banking and wealth management. Company-specific risks also appear to be playing a role in the downward pressure. Concerns regarding elevated private-market markdown risk have been voiced, potentially affecting Goldman Sachs’ exposures in these areas and its asset management portfolios. Adding to this sentiment, recent reports highlighted that a number of company insiders sold shares, which can sometimes be interpreted as a negative signal to the market. While there was some positive news earlier in the month regarding the company's strategic expansion into stable fee-based businesses and increased dealmaking expectations, the pervasive macroeconomic and geopolitical uncertainties appear to be outweighing these factors in the short term. The overall market is reacting to increased volatility and a cautious outlook, with equity indices under pressure due to the unresolved conflict. The RSI at 43.00 suggests neutral condition and the Williams %R at -43.25 suggests oversold condition. Please monitor closely. title: A Look At Goldman Sachs (GS) Valuation As Recession Odds Rise On Oil Shock And Geopolitical Tensions - simplywall.st
content:
Goldman Sachs Group (GS) shares have moved recently after the bank raised its U.S. recession probability to 30%, tying the shift to Middle East war related oil supply shocks, higher inflation expectations, and delayed Federal Reserve rate cuts. Those more cautious recession comments come as GS has given back ground in recent weeks, with a 1 month share price return of 8.83% and a 90 day share price return of 9.30%. However, the 1 year total shareholder return of 50.23% points to strong longer term momentum that is now cooling. If you are looking beyond large banks for what is setting the tone in markets right now, this is a good moment to scan the 20 cryptocurrency and blockchain stocks With shares about 10% lower year to date but still up 50.23% over 1 year, plus a roughly 10% intrinsic discount and a 16.67% gap to the average analyst target, is there still a buying opportunity here, or is future growth already priced in? Goldman Sachs Group’s most followed narrative pegs fair value at $916, compared with the last close at $822.64, which puts a clear valuation gap in focus. The primary engine for Goldman's valuation in 2026 is the strategic simplification of its business model. The "real story" is the firm's exit from the high-friction consumer credit market to double down on the high-margin "Physical AI" infrastructure wave. Goldman is no longer trying to be a bank for everyone; it is once again the "foundry" for the world's largest capital transactions. By positioning itself as the primary architect for the $760 million-per-day CapEx spend of AI hyperscalers, Goldman has turned a macro tech trend into a direct investment banking fee machine, allowing it to guide for a record $57.70 EPS for fiscal 2026. Want to see what sits behind that fair value call? The narrative leans heavily on fee rich advisory, ambitious earnings power, and a profit profile usually reserved for premium franchises. title: NorAm Finals Aspen: GS Sweeps, Slalom Battles, WC Spots Set - Ski Racing Media
content: Featured Image: Amelia Smart 🇨🇦 charges to victory in the Day 1 women’s slalom at the NorAm circuit. Photo by Eric Brandolini. Aspen Highlands set the stage for NorAm Finals, where World Cup veterans and NCAA champions collided with World Cup start positions on the line. How and when to watch. In this article you will find NorAm Finals GS and SL results, field analysis, and the athletes who secured World Cup start opportunities. Amelia Smart delivered a composed second run to take the win. The University of Denver standout and three-time NCAA champion managed difficult conditions by staying centered and aggressive, saying, “I knew it was pretty tough conditions, so I just focused on staying balanced and still pushing the speed as much as I could. I’m glad it paid off.” She added that returning to Colorado carried extra meaning, calling it “like coming back to my second home.” Franziska Gritsch, a seasoned World Cup veteran, used her experience to stay close in second and set the tone for the series. Justine Lamontagne, coming off a 2026 NCAA double title in GS and SL, continued her strong form with a third-place finish. Just behind, Kjersti Moritz led the Americans in fourth, while Arianne Forget added another strong result for Alpine Canada. Franziska Gritsch used eight seasons of World Cup experience to control the race and take the win. Madison Hoffman, a University of Utah standout and NCAA double champion in GS and SL (2023), showed powerful, confident skiing to finish second. Eleri Smart continued the University of Denver pipeline with a composed run to third, earning her first career NorAm podium, while her sister Amelia Smart, a three-time NCAA champion, finished just behind in fourth. Justine Lamontagne, one of the most decorated NCAA athletes in the field, capped her collegiate career at Montana State with a 2026 NCAA double title in GS and SL, and rounded out the top five. Although 🇦🇹 Gritsch secured the NorAm SL title, World Cup start positions go to the top North Americans Both women’s NorAm Finals giant slalom races in Aspen were canceled due to warm temperatures, preventing any additional results from impacting the standings. As a result, Franziska Gritsch (AUT, 1997) secured the NorAm giant slalom title based on prior results. However, World Cup start positions are awarded to the top North American athletes in the standings for the 2027 World Cup season: At just 17 years old, Grosdidier, the youngest member of the Stifel U.S. Ski Team, earns a World Cup start opportunity for the 2027 season, marking a significant step forward in his progression. Forget, already showing consistency across disciplines, adds a World Cup GS start to her résumé for the 2027 season following a strong NorAm campaign. Franziska Gritsch 🇦🇹 (AUT, 1997) secured the women’s NorAm overall title, capping a consistent season across disciplines. However, as a non-North American, Gritsch does not take a World Cup quota position. The top North American finisher, Logan Grosdidier 🇺🇸 (USA, 2008) of the Stifel U.S. Ski Team, delivered one of the standout performances of the season. At just 17 years old, Grosdidier has earned NorAm World Cup start positions in all four disciplines for the 2027 season, highlighting both her versatility and rapid progression across speed and technical events. Bridger Gile, skiing on home snow in Aspen, opened the series with a composed and controlled performance. The Stifel U.S. Ski Team World Cup veteran used his familiarity with the terrain to take the win, saying “it meant a lot to do it at home.” Johs Braathen Herland, the University of Utah standout and two-time NCAA GS champion (2025, 2026), showed strong pace to finish second and set the tone for his eventual overall title. Patrick Kenney, another Stifel U.S. Ski Team World Cup veteran and former University of New Hampshire All-American, delivered a consistent performance for third. Bridger Gile completed a sweep in Aspen, combining strength and efficiency across both runs to secure a second victory. “Winning one race is hard, so to win two days in a row is a super sweet feeling,” he said. He entered the series carrying speed and confidence built over the past month, noting “I’ve been skiing fast since the beginning of February. This was just the moment to put it together.” The Highlands track added another layer to the performance, with Gile describing it as “a really cool hill — it’s got pitch, terrain, but also really good flow.” Patrick Kenney continued his consistent form in second, while Isaiah Nelson, a rising member of the Stifel U.S. Ski Team, secured third and a key World Cup opportunity. Although 🇳🇴 Herland secured the overall NorAm GS title, World Cup start positions go to the top North Americans Cooper Puckett delivered a composed performance to take the win, holding off a tight field. The Stifel U.S. Ski Team athlete has already competed in seven World Cup slaloms this season and scored in his very first World Cup start in Levi, showing the level he is now bringing to the NorAm circuit. Just behind, Jevin Palmquist missed the win by just four hundredths. The rising American, who finished fourth at the 2026 FIS Junior World Ski Championships in slalom, continues to show he can compete at the front against both established and experienced athletes. Matej Vidović, the NorAm slalom title winner, added another podium with a strong second run, while Maximilien Hoder—a World Cup start qualifier—and Stanley Buzek rounded out a tightly packed top five. Jevin Palmquist responded one day after narrowly missing victory, delivering a composed performance to take the win by just one hundredth. The American continues to build momentum after a fourth-place finish at the 2026 FIS Junior World Championships in slalom, showing he can execute under pressure at the highest level of this field. Matej Vidović, the NorAm slalom title winner, pushed hard again and finished just off the top step, missing the win by the smallest margin of the week. In third, Étienne Mazellier, out of Stoneham, Canada, delivered a strong, balanced performance to secure the podium and continue Canada’s depth in the discipline. Cooper Puckett, fresh off his win on March 25, backed it up with another solid result in fourth, while Bradshaw Underhill rounded out the top five in a tightly contested race. The 2026 NorAm slalom title was shared by Matej Vidović 🇭🇷 (CRO, 1993, Head) and Maximilien Hoder 🇺🇸 (USA, 2005), marking a tie at the top between the experienced Croatian and one of the rising American talents. However, World Cup start positions are awarded to the top North American athletes in the standings for the 2027 World Cup season: Both athletes represent the next generation of the Stifel U.S. Ski Team pipeline, with Hoder coming through Mount Mansfield Ski Academy and Palmquist developing with Team Summit. Their results underline the continued strength of the American development system, with young athletes stepping into World Cup start opportunities. Johs Braathen Hæland 🇳🇴 (NOR, 2003) of the University of Utah secured the overall NorAm title, continuing a dominant season that included a GS title and multiple podium performances. However, as a non-North American, Hæland does not take a World Cup quota position. The top North American finisher, Isaiah Nelson 🇺🇸 (USA, 2001) of the Stifel U.S. Ski Team, emerged as one of the biggest stories of the season. Nelson has earned NorAm World Cup start positions in all four disciplines for the 2027 season, highlighting both his versatility and progression across speed and technical events. title: One of the best restaurants in L.A. is reopening inside the Fairmont this summer - Time Out Worldwide
content: Thanks for subscribing! Look out for your first newsletter in your inbox soon! We help you navigate a myriad of possibilities. Sign up for our newsletter for the best of the city. By entering your email address you agree to our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy and consent to receive emails from Time Out about news, events, offers and partner promotions. Thanks for subscribing! By entering your email address you agree to our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy and consent to receive emails from Time Out about news, events, offers and partner promotions. One of L.A.’s most beloved modern comfort food restaurants is making a comeback with a major new address. Birdie G’s, the acclaimed concept from chef Jeremy Fox, is set to reopen in summer 2026 inside the landmark Fairmont Century Plaza in Century City. RECOMMENDED: One of L.A.’s best restaurants is closing at the end of the year The announcement marks a welcome return for the restaurant, which closed its original Santa Monica location at Bergamot Station at the end of 2025 after a six-year run that earned it a devoted following and frequent placement on the Los Angeles Times’ 101 Best Restaurants list. Now, it’s stepping into a much bigger spotlight, taking over the hotel’s current dining space and expanding into a full-scale, all-day brasserie. “Birdie G’s has always been about capturing the feeling of a classic American restaurant through the lens of memory and craft,” said Chef Jeremy Fox in a statement. “At Fairmont Century Plaza, we’re expanding that vision into a true all‑day experience—from early‑morning coffee to celebratory dinners—while staying rooted in the comfort and creativity that define the restaurant.” If the original Birdie G’s felt like a deeply personal project (Fox named it for his daughter and grandmother), this new iteration aims to scale that sentiment without losing its soul. Breakfast will lean into deli-inspired classics, house-made pastries and coffee service geared toward both hotel guests and early business meetings. Lunch splits the difference between quick and polished, nodding to Century City’s legacy of dealmaking over midday meals. By dinner, the menu settles into something more expansive: steaks, chops, fish and handmade pastas, alongside the vegetable-forward dishes that have become Fox’s calling card. Regulars will find plenty to celebrate. Signature hits like the matzo ball soup, the Pickle Chick cutlet and the rose petal pie are all slated to reappear, joined by koji-aged steaks and new dishes developed specifically for the hotel setting. As before, the kitchen will prioritize seasonal sourcing from local farmers and a low-waste approach to cooking. The beverage program follows suit, balancing classics with more seasonal, ingredient-driven creations. A wine list focused on sustainable producers and low-intervention practices rounds things out, alongside craft beer and nonalcoholic options. Design-wise, the space is being reimagined in collaboration with EDG Design, drawing inspiration from mid-century business culture and classic American supper clubs, and tuned to match Century Plaza’s long-standing role as a hub for both Hollywood glamour and corporate power dining. With the hotel’s 60th anniversary approaching, the arrival of Birdie G’s positions it to welcome a new era. And for a restaurant that was widely mourned when it closed, the reopening is a glow-up, with a dining room ready for everything from early coffee meetings to late-night celebrations. title: Coach G’s Corner: Iowa State - Local 3 News
content: The Mocs are in the midst of their first week of spring prac… On Wednesday night, Red Bank and Notre Dame met for non-dist… Cleveland's Grant Hurst helps us breakdown Tennessee's Sweet 16 match-up against Iowa State on Friday. Another round of the NCAA Tournament and another breakdown with Cleveland native, Grant Hurst. Iowa State is similar to Tennessee in how they pride themselves on defense. They also love to turn their defense into offense. Coach G believes limiting turnovers will be a major key. He says the team usually has a mindset of no more than ten. It will be even more vital to keep that number in double digits on Friday night. He also believes some big games from their forwards will be a factor. The guys down low have been playing great throughout their tournament run. They’ll need another solid performance against the Cyclones. Coach G also has a lot of faith in freshman Amari Evans. The Pittsburgh native may be called upon to handle, and protect, the ball a lot in the game. Coach G also thinks a big night shooting from Evans can be a difference maker. Tennessee and Iowa State are set to tip-off at 10:10 p.m. ET on Friday in the Sweet 16. title: GS Caltex Defeats Hyundai Engineering & Construction, Silva 40 - 조선일보
content: Cuban star's 40 points, key serves propel GS Caltex to 1-0 lead in best-of-three playoffs GS Caltex of the women's professional volleyball league successfully took the initiative against Hyundai Engineering & Construction, the regular league runner-up, led by foreign player Gisele Silva, Cuba, in the V-League playoffs. GS Caltex defeated Hyundai Engineering & Construction 3-1 (25-21, 21-25, 25-23, 25-16) in the first playoff game held at Suwon Gymnasium on the 26th. In the women's division, the team that won the first game in all 19 previous playoffs (best-of-three format) advanced to the championship finals. GS Caltex, which came from third place in the regular league and advanced through the preliminary playoffs, has taken a favorable position. Silva, who scored over 1,000 points for three consecutive years in the regular league, continued her explosive attacking power in the postseason. After scoring 42 points in the preliminary playoffs against Heungkuk Life, she led the team to victory with 40 points on the day. In the crucial third set, trailing 22-21 in a close situation, she extended the lead with an ace serve and finished the set with a diagonal smash at 24-23 set point. Meanwhile, Korea Expressway Corporation, which advanced directly to the championship finals as the regular league champion, effectively dismissed Coach Kim Jong-min on the same day. The corporation stated, "We highly appreciate the leadership Coach Kim has shown over the past 10 years," but added, "However, due to the unfortunate incident of being summarily indicted for alleged assault on a coach, we have decided after careful consideration not to renew his contract." Coach Kim's contract is set to expire on the 31st of this month. The championship finals, starting on the 1st of next month, will be led by the head coach.

title: The new Broadband Battery from GS Yuasa Energy Solutions Provides High Performance and Long Life Even in the Hottest Climates - PR Newswire
content: In-Language News ROSWELL, Ga., March 26, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- GS Yuasa Energy Solutions Inc. (GYES) announces the release of the new GS Yuasa (GSY) Broadband high-temperature battery for cable TV markets with integrated handles for easy and safe installation. Compared to competitive offerings, it offers longer life, even in the extremes of uncontrolled environments. Known as the PYB battery, it is built using the same HT X Alloy™ lead purity standard and long-life features found in other GSY lead products. Independent testing of the GSY lead products continue to demonstrate that these products last longer. With greater than ten years of design life at 25°C (more than five years at temperatures of 35°C), this lead technology is already in broad use with Tier 1 telecommunications carriers. "We're excited to bring GS Yuasa's proven, best‑in‑class lead battery technology to the CATV market. Our new PYB Broadband batteries are built with the same proven, consistent, and reliable quality that defines our Telecom (PWL/PYL) and UPS (SWU) Series products," said Bill Moll, President of GYES. "The HT X Alloy used across our product portfolio delivers the long service life demanded in these high‑temperature environments." HT X Alloy maintains tightly controlled trace elements that extend lifespan in high temperature environments. It enhances other long-life features like their proprietary additives, calcium to add strength to lead plate grids, and tin to improve bonding. The result is a high-temperature lead battery with exceptional lead purity standards with optimized performance and life. The PYB battery meets or exceeds industry standards for AGM/VRLA batteries and will start shipping in the US market starting April 2026. Contact us today and begin having your battery investment last longer. PYB12V110TT Spec Sheet About GYES: GS Yuasa is a Japanese corporation known for quality and global ﬁrsts. GS Yuasa globally produces batteries, in different chemistries, serving many markets and applications. Our advanced technologies have proven successes in American markets with Lithium and Lead-acid in Telecom, UPS, Alarm (Fire & Security), Energy Storage, Emergency Lighting, and other applications.

title: Birdie G’s returns: Chef Jeremy Fox to open new flagship at Fairmont Century Plaza - smmirror.com
content:
Following its December closure in Santa Monica, the acclaimed California restaurant returns this summer. The best news for fans of Chef Jeremy Fox and his restaurant Birdie G’s has arrived. After what seemed to be the last installment of 8 Nights at Birdie G’s, the incredible eight-day-long chef collaboration at the restaurant, Birdie G’s, closed on December 20, 2025. There was no hinting that the restaurant might return, so it was a sad farewell. But, wait, that wasn’t the end of the story. It was announced today that Chef Fox will bring the beloved Santa Monica favorite to Century City. The Fairmont Century City hotel posted on its Instagram page that Chef Fox would be leading a revival of Birdie G’s as an all-day restaurant at the hotel this summer, replacing the current restaurant, Lumiere. The post said, “@birdiegsla, the acclaimed concept from @chefjeremyfox, will open as the hotel’s new signature restaurant—bringing its deeply personal, seasonal produce-driven approach to Century City. Birdie G’s at Fairmont Century Plaza will introduce breakfast, brunch, lunch, and dinner service, offering a full spectrum of cuisine that blends comfort, creativity, and a distinctly California perspective within a modern American brasserie setting.” The Fairmont’s account added, “The arrival marks a vibrant new chapter for dining in Century City,” and we couldn’t agree more. This wonderful news comes two weeks after it was announced that Chef Fox would become the  Culinary Director and Partner at The Restaurant at Edna and See You at Edna, starting in the summer in San Luis Obispo. title: GS Caltex Secures 100% Final Chance with Silva's Dominance - 조선일보
content: Silva scores 40 points as GS Caltex defeats Hyundai Engineering & Construction 3-1 in playoff opener [OSEN=Hong Jisu Reporter] GS Caltex secured a 100% probability of advancing to the championship finals after defeating Hyundai Engineering & Construction in the first round. Gisele Silva (registered as Silva) once again played a pivotal role. GS Caltex defeated Hyundai Engineering & Construction with a set score of 3-1 (25-21, 21-25, 25-23, 25-17) in the first round of the Jin Air 2025-2026 V-League women’s playoff held at Suwon Indoor Gymnasium on the 26th. The team that wins the PO 1st round has a 100% chance of advancing to the championship finals. Silva dominated the match once again. Silva, who scored 42 points in the playoff qualifier against Heungkuk Life Insurance held at Jangchung Gymnasium on the 24th, recorded 40 points in the playoff 1st round. Even when anticipated, she was unstoppable. Moreover, the playoff 1st round took place just one day after the playoff qualifier. Although physically demanding, Silva showed no signs of fatigue. She successfully scored double-digit points in every set, acting as the key player. Reina, who was substituted in, also contributed 7 points. Hyundai Engineering & Construction saw balanced performances from Kari (19 points), Justis (15 points), and Lee Ye-rim (10 points), but failed to stop GS Caltex’s momentum. Additionally, their unforced errors (25) exceeded GS Caltex’s (20), and the game did not go smoothly. From the first set, GS Caltex took control amid a tight flow. After Oh Se-yeon’s serve point and Choi Ga-eun’s block at 9-9, GS expanded the lead with Silva’s consecutive points. At 21-18, Silva added a serve point, fully seizing the momentum. Silva recorded 10 points in the first set alone, achieving a 52.9% attack success rate. In the second set, Hyundai Engineering & Construction successfully counterattacked. Kari revived the atmosphere with consecutive points. However, Lee Ye-rim’s open attack at 24-21 tied the set score to 1-1. But GS Caltex captured the third set led by Silva and easily secured the fourth set. Silva’s open attacks, Kim Mi-yeon’s contributions, and Choi Ga-eun’s quick attacks completely disrupted Hyundai Engineering & Construction. The two teams will face off in the playoff 2nd round at Seoul Jangchung Gymnasium on the 28th.

title: GS MODULAR to Debut at the 139th Canton Fair, Leading the Future of Modular Buildings - 24-7 Press Release Newswire
content:
GUANGZHOU, CHINA, March 26, 2026 /24-7PressRelease/ -- From April 23 to April 27, 2026, GS MODULAR, a global provider of modular construction solutions, will showcase its latest permanent modular building solutions at the 139th China Import and Export Fair (Canton Fair) in Guangzhou, China (Booth No.: Hall 12.1, J15–16).

About GS MODULAR
GS MODULAR specializes in the R&D, design, and manufacturing of modular building systems. The company is dedicated to providing clients worldwide with efficient, sustainable, and highly customized modular construction solutions.

Leveraging extensive experience accumulated in modular construction technologies, GS MODULAR serves the global market by delivering permanent modular building products for a wide spectrum of modular homes, modular hotel, commercial, and specialized project applications.

This presentation fully demonstrates the company's unwavering commitment to providing the global market with efficient, durable, and sustainable construction solutions.We warmly invite global clients and partners to visit us at Booth J15–16, Hall 12.1, to discover the company's latest permanent modular construction solutions.

Industry Background:
As the global construction industry faces increasing pressure to deliver projects with shorter timelines, greater efficiency, and enhanced sustainability, modular construction has emerged as one of the fastest-growing trends in the modern building sector.

By integrating factory-based manufacturing, standardized production, and modern architectural design, modular construction significantly reduces construction cycles while maintaining high structural quality and superior performance. As governments and developers worldwide seek more efficient construction methods, modular construction solutions are gaining increasingly widespread recognition and adoption.

As one of the world's largest international trade exhibitions, the Canton Fair provides a vital platform for global enterprises to connect with buyers, developers, and construction industry professionals. GS MODULAR aims to leverage this grand event to further strengthen its international partnerships and expand its footprint within the global modular construction market.

Product Highlights
At the 139th Canton Fair, GS MODULAR will place a special focus on showcasing its permanent modular building systems—systems specifically engineered for long-term use and constructed in strict compliance with international building standards.

These modular structures combine high-strength structural frameworks, modern architectural design concepts, and advanced prefabrication technologies, thereby ensuring the buildings' durability, safety, and exceptional longevity. GS MODULAR's modular construction solutions are widely applicable across a diverse range of building types, including:
·modular homes
·Modular Apartment
·Commercial Buildings
·Resorts and Hotels
·Educational and Healthcare Facilities

Through factory prefabrication and on-site modular assembly, these structures achieve shorter construction cycles, stricter quality control, and greater design flexibility, thereby serving as highly efficient solutions for modern construction projects.

Executive Quote
Zhang Guiping, CEO of GS MODULAR, said, "We are committed to advancing innovation and quality in modular construction, delivering flexible and sustainable modular building solutions that support the global construction industry in moving toward a smarter and greener future. We will continue strengthening our technology and service capabilities, striving to become a trusted modular housing system service provider."

We warmly invite customers and partners attending the 139th Canton Fair to visit our booth—Hall 12.1, Booth J15–16—to gain deeper insights into GS MODULAR's modular construction solutions and to explore potential collaboration opportunities.# # #

title: GS Engineering & Construction CEO Advances Physical AI in Construction - 조선일보
content: CEO Huh highlights practical execution, innovation through physical AI to transform construction with robots and AI-designed projects GS Engineering & Construction CEO Huh Yoon-hong emphasized the transition to ‘physical AI (artificial intelligence)’ at an executive workshop. Physical AI refers to AI being implemented in physical bodies or forms such as robots, directly impacting the real world. GS Engineering & Construction stated that it discussed ideas for applying physical AI to the field at an executive workshop held over two days starting the previous day on the 26th. About 110 executives from GS Engineering & Construction and its subsidiaries, including CEO Huh Yoon-hong, attended. The executives were divided into four groups to discuss the review of physical AI adoption, practical on-site usage, support from business departments and the entire company, and sustainable organizational management systems when introducing robots. CEO Huh emphasized, “The outcome of this workshop is not just ideas but actual execution that changes the field,” and stressed, “Instead of creating a perfect answer from the start, we should quickly try, improve, verify on-site, and proceed to the next step.” He added, “I expect the discussions from this workshop to function practically on-site, be shared with members, and lead to substantial changes,” and stated, “We will secure the company’s competitiveness by innovating the field centered on physical AI.” GS Engineering & Construction has applied on-site AI translation programs like ‘Xi Voice, 자이 보이스’ for smooth communication with foreign workers, ‘Xi-Book, 자이북’ which informs engineers of the latest standards through AI for over 5,000 pages of standard specifications, and the industry’s first AI-based design drawing review system. A source from GS Engineering & Construction said, “Beyond introducing physical AI this year, we will establish a new construction paradigm where designs are created with AI and construction is carried out with robots.”

title: Warriors 109-106 Nets (26 Mar, 2026) Game Recap - ESPN
content: Brooklyn Nets vs. Golden State Warriors: Game Highlights SAN FRANCISCO -- — Gui Santos scored a career-high 31 points, Draymond Green made two clutch free throws with 6.9 seconds remaining and the Golden State Warriors beat the Brooklyn Nets 109-106 on Wednesday to clinch a play-in spot. Brandin Podziemski had 22 points, six rebounds and five assists for the Warriors (35-38), who won their first game at Chase Center since returning from a grueling 2-4 road trip. Kristaps Porzingis added 17 points and De’Anthony Melton had 14, including a pair of free throws in the final seconds, as Golden State beat Brooklyn in San Francisco for the first time since Dec. 16, 2023. Ziaire Williams had 19 points for the Nets, who have lost nine straight. Jalen Wilson added 15 points off the bench while Ben Saraf had 14 points and seven rebounds. The sellout at Chase Center was the 600th consecutive sellout for the Warriors, the sixth-longest streak in NBA history, The Nets led most of the game, trailed going into the fourth then wore down over the final 12 minutes. Brooklyn shot 8 for 20 (2 for 9 behind the arc) down the stretch. Still without injured star Stephen Curry, the Warriors committed 15 turnovers in the first two quarters and had trouble running their offense with much consistency. The Nets also got off to a sluggish start and missed 10 of their first 15 shots before Williams warmed up. The former first-round draft pick repeatedly attacked through the paint and scored 11 points to help Brooklyn to a 58-50 halftime lead. Golden State pulled within 63-62 midway through the third before Powell made consecutive 3-pointers to get Brooklyn on track. Nets: Face the Lakers in Los Angeles on Friday. title: Birdie G’s Will Reopen at Fairmont Century Plaza - lamag.com
content: The beloved restaurant from chef Jeremy Fox — that closed in Dec. 2025 — is joining the Century City hotel After it opened in June 2019, chef Jeremy Fox‘s Birdie G’s quickly became a beloved landmark in the Santa Monica dining scene, gaining admiration from critics and leaving customers craving more. Then, on December 31, 2025, it permanently closed its doors. But fans are in luck: This summer, chef Fox (also formerly of Rustic Canyon) will bring his nostalgic American cuisine mixed with unique design to Fairmont Century Plaza’s revamped premises. This new location, in partnership with EDG Design, will serve as a gathering place for Los Angeles residents and hotel guests alike to come together over good traditional food, conversation and celebration. “As we celebrate nearly six decades of history at Fairmont Century Plaza, we are proud to welcome Birdie G’s as the new cornerstone of the hotel’s dining program,” said Hiren Prabhakar, General Manager of Fairmont Century Plaza. “Century Plaza has always been defined by great dining as well as great events, and Birdie G’s honors that heritage while bringing renewed energy to the heart of the hotel.” Birdie G’s was originally named after chef Fox’s daughter Birdie, as well as his grandmother Gladys, as an homage to his family. Birdie G’s is known for its unique and effective to serving American comfort food in style and for six years, it built a beloved brand. The menu is nothing short of drool-worthy and exactly what you might be craving for breakfast, lunch, brunch and dinner service. Here, patrons can enjoy a wide variety of elevated yet classic dishes throughout the day. Inspired by mid-century business culture, the venue’s warm tones, comfortable banquette seating and expanded patio dining will greet diners. “Birdie G’s has always been about capturing the feeling of a classic American restaurant through the lens of memory and craft,” said chef Fox. “At Fairmont Century Plaza, we’re expanding that vision into a true all‑day experience — from early‑morning coffee to celebratory dinners — while staying rooted in the comfort and creativity that define the restaurant.” Weekend brunch is not to be missed and is an occasion for sharing dishes. Dinner guests can choose from an elegant display of steaks, chops, poultry and fish, alongside handmade pastas and seasonal vegetable dishes that cater to California’s seasons. Birdie G’s fans should not worry, as some of their most celebrated dishes such as the aromatic matzo ball soup, the beloved Pickle Chick cutlet and the signature rose petal pie will be revived. But, alongside them will be new additions exclusively for Fairmont Century Plaza. The cocktail menu will combine modern and classic options, with seasonal ingredients. Wines come from chemical-free vineyards and tout sustainable winemaking and farming practices. Or, sip craft beers or pick from a thoughtful selection of non-alcoholic offerings. The announcement arrives as Fairmont Century Plaza approaches its 60th anniversary, marking six decades of making history. From Grammy celebrations to presidential gatherings, the landmark hotel continues to evolve while honoring its storied past. Additional details, including reservation information and opening festivities, will be announced in the weeks leading up to Birdie G’s debut. To learn more about the hotel’s events or to make a reservation, visit www.fairmontcenturyplaza.com or call 310-424-3030. Plus, Madhappy debuts in Malibu and Chrissy Teigen’s checkout takeover at Ralph’s

Ten top undergraduate students majoring in supply chain management at Georgia Southern University recently traveled to Fort Collins, CO, to participate in the longest running supply chain student case competition. The 2026 field of 19 teams from 18 different colleges and universities across the U.S. analyzed and presented recommendations for a fast-fashion retailer’s inbound freight transportation needs. The case, developed with FreightWaves data and insights, required students to develop a transportation budget that also met delivery speed and risk mitigation requirements for thousands of loads throughout the coming year. Teams decided the mix of truckload and rail modalities as well as a truckload contract versus spot rate alternatives for 36 major freight lanes connected to major import gateways of Southern California and Savannah. Students representing Georgia Southern and the Department of Logistics and SCM in the Parker College of Business won their bracket and reached the final four of the event. The team performed well in the final round against Colorado State, Bowling Green and Western Michigan, with Colorado State eventually claiming the top spot.

title: Quick Bites: Bella G's Pizza & Pasta House - The Retrospect
content: With daffodils in full bloom and perfect weather over the weekend, the Jersey Diner crew took the opportunity to gather and enjoy some outdoor activities. And what goes better with badminton and cornhole than pizza? This week we picked up from Bella G's Pizza & Pasta House in Bellmawr. Excited for wings, we started with 20 of them coated in barbecue sauce. The crew felt these were delicious, crispy, juicy and meaty with a good amount of sauce. We really couldn’t ask for anything more. We got these with a side of fries, which were standard but stayed crispy even after transport and cooling down, which we will take as a win! As for pizza, this was another hit. While you’d be hard pressed to find a truly bad pizza in South Jersey, we particularly enjoyed the thicker crust Bella G’s uses on their pies. We found that both their plain cheese pizza and tomato pie had perfectly cooked crust, crispy and chewy with big bubbles. We also felt the sauce tasted fresh and garlicky, which is extra noticeable on their tomato pie. If you’re more of a sandwich person, we thought the classic cheese steak was delicious. Stuffed with correctly cooked and tender shaved steak, we thought the ratio of cheese, steak and bread was perfect. Similarly, the American hoagie tasted freshly made with enough toppings to fill you up quickly. One crew member in particular noted they enjoyed the thinly sliced onion on top, which added to the freshness. This sandwich and the cheesesteak both had particularly good bread, which is essential for the yumminess of a sandwich. We will be back again!

title: Birdie G’s Comes Back to Life as an All-Day Restaurant in Century City - Eater Los Angeles
content: ﻿The beloved Santa Monica restaurant will reopen this summer at Fairmont Century Plaza If you buy something from a link, Vox Media may earn a commission. See our ethics statement. ﻿The beloved Santa Monica restaurant will reopen this summer at Fairmont Century Plaza If you buy something from a link, Vox Media may earn a commission. See our ethics statement. Jeremy Fox’s beloved Santa Monica restaurant, Birdie G’s, will come back to life at the Fairmont Century Plaza hotel in Century City. The Midwestern and Jewish–inspired restaurant, which closed at the end of 2025, is set to reopen at the hotel in summer 2026. Lumière, the Fairmont’s current restaurant, will close in May to make way for Birdie G’s. Add Eater as a preferred source to see more of our reporting on Google. Birdie G’s will revive as an all day restaurant, serving breakfast through dinner for hotel guests and local diners alike. Mornings will bring coffee and pastries for dine-in or grab-and-go as part of a delicatessen-influenced menu. Daytime service arrives with a full menu meant for more relaxed mid-day meals alongside abbreviated options better suited for a power lunch. For dinner, expect the return of Birdie G’s favorites including matzo ball soup, the gluten-free Pickle Chick, rose petal pie, and koji-aged steaks, alongside new dishes exclusive to the Century City location. The restaurant will also add a weekend brunch of steaks, pastas, and seasonal vegetables, and serve cocktails as well as beer and wine. EDG Design will lead the reimagining of the Lumière space, pulling influence from American brasseries. Expect the resurrected restaurant to feature warm tones, banquettes, and patio seating. Fox, the founding chef of Rustic Canyon and author of cookbook On Vegetables, initially opened Birdie G’s in Santa Monica in 2019 at the edge of Bergamot Station Arts Center. The restaurant took its name from Fox’s daughter Birdie and grandmother Gladys. Its 2025 closure came amid ongoing challenges for the restaurant with the 2023 writers’ strike, the Eaton and Palisades fires, and a drop in tourism in Santa Monica. Alongside the reopening of Birdie G’s, Fox will also work with San Luis Obispo’s Edna, a forthcoming restaurant, cafe, market, and distillery, as a partner and culinary director. Matthew Schaler, Birdie G’s former executive chef, joins Fox at Edna, which will open in phases beginning this spring. Update: March 26, 2026, 9:25 a.m.: This story was updated with a new closure date for Lumière. title: Stunning Shiffrin crowned Overall champion as Scheib delights in GS Globe - fis-ski.com
content: Some of the data collected by this provider is for the purposes of personalization and measuring advertising effectiveness. Some of the data collected by this provider is for the purposes of personalization and measuring advertising effectiveness. A stunning day and a sensational season of women’s Audi FIS Alpine Ski World Cup racing came to a thrilling conclusion in Hafjell on Wednesday, with Mikaela Shiffrin (USA/Atomic) equalling childhood hero Annemarie Moser-Pröll (AUT) by winning a record sixth Overall Crystal Globe. But the brilliant Emma Aicher (GER/Head) made her work mighty hard for it. Needing to win the Giant Slalom and for Shiffrin to finish outside the top-15 in order to snatch the coveted title away, the German turned the pressure valve up to the max at the halfway stage. A stunning first run put her third while an understandably tense Shiffrin was sat 17th. The steal was on. Shiffrin, however, was having none of it. Knowing any misstep would open the door to her rival, the American went into overdrive. Intense but smooth, Shiffrin flew down the Hafjell hill second time around and into the history books. While Shiffrin confirmed her spot as the greatest Overall skier of the season, there is zero doubt that Julia Scheib (AUT/Rossignol) is currently in her own league when it comes to the GS. Winner of five of the 10 GS races, the Austrian ended third on the day, before lifting the GS Globe for the first time in her career. Ahead of the Austrian on Wednesday came Mina Fuerst Holtmann (NOR/Atomic) who gave the home crowd plenty to shout about with her best result of the season. While Valerie Grenier (CAN/Rossignol) finished out in front, as she secured Canada’s first victory of the season in any discipline, winning by a big 0.43-second margin. Shiffrin may not have dominated across the disciplines to the extent she did in her extraordinary 2018/19 and 2022/23 seasons, but this has undoubtedly been one of her most impressive campaigns. After returning from her horror abdomen injury in late January 2025, the American started this campaign full of doubts. But able to lean on her untouchable Slalom skills, the 31-year-old built solidly into the GS, eventually recording more top-six finishes than anyone else, as she landed a title she has long eyed up. Although she took some convincing that a sixth Big Globe is finally hers. The American legend is certainly free to celebrate, another Overall Globe sitting alongside the Slalom Globe she landed back in January. Although her nerves were pushed to the limit on the final day of the season. “I mean I was in 17th position after the first run, and you don't get points in 17th here. So, I really could appreciate that this day could be different,” Shiffrin said. “It was a lot of effort to push on the second and I was really happy with my second run, much better timing. I felt much more fluid and just like kind of relaxed, even though I was actually quite stressed.” An incredible return of 980 points out of a possible 1000 in the Slalom allied to her strong GS efforts – including a podium in Spindleruv Mlyn – plus two top 23 finishes in Super G delivered a final tally of 1,410 points. Just 87 clear of Aicher. “I have to say to Emma that her skiing has been just outstanding, and today, it was just so cool to watch her, especially on the first run. I really thought the outcome of this day is that she could do this. And that's the coolest thing about ski racing, that anything is possible.” The 22-year-old Aicher ends her breathtaking season second in the Overall standings, second in Downhill, third in Super G, sixth in Slalom and having recorded her best ever result in the GS (fourth in Are). Add on a pair of Olympic silver medals – won in the Downhill and the Team Combined, in which she skied the Slalom leg – and it is no surprise one of the sport’s greatest all-rounders has been cheering on happily from the sidelines. “I am extremely impressed by Emma’s performance this season, combining speed and slalom disciplines is very, very hard which is why almost no one is doing it anymore,” double Alpine Combined Olympic champion Michelle Gisin (SUI/Salomon) said. “I think she just needs to keep doing what she is doing right now, it’s just perfect.” As for the racer herself, she is just loving being able to compete at the very top every time she straps the skis on. Five wins and eight podiums shows she definitely has it. “It's been a really fun one, also just to keep the fight going until the very last race, it's pretty unbelievable,” she said. “It’s been so much fun racing and just skiing.” Scheib has made it fun for all her supporters, finding a top pace in the GS that no one else came near to. The Globe a fitting reward for an utterly dominant season. All this after starting the season back on 25 October having never climbed to the top step of a World Cup podium in her previous 2832 days as an elite ski racer. “A season I will remember for ever,” said Scheib, the first Austrian to lift the GS Globe since Eva-Maria Brem (AUT) more than a decade ago. Rival Rast will also head to the beach with a big smile on her face. Second in the GS and Slalom Globes, Olympic Slalom silver and third in the World Cup Overall is quite an array of honours. “Fighting with only two disciplines for the Overall is quite amazing, I am really happy about the consistency in every race,” Rast said, before turning her attention to getting even better. “I will try to find some little solutions for next season. I hope to be fit 100% and we go again for the 1000 points.” Sara Hector (SWE/Head) was fourth on the day in Hafjell, enough to keep her third in the GS Globe standings for the third season in-a-row. That is a level of consistency neither the Hafjell runner-up nor the winner can yet match, but both will come back next season far stronger after finishing in style in the Norwegian sunshine. “It’s fantastic," Holtmann said. "I am so happy to get this now after a tough season. It gives me a lot of joy and a lot of motivation." A first podium in the GS since December 2019 for Holtmann, while for Grenier it is a first win for more than two years. Check out the full results from the women’s GS in Hafjell here, with the final women’s Globe standings here. Also, do please head to Reuters Connect and Actionpress.de to view and purchase a wide selection of photographs from this race and from all FIS events.

title: Mikaela Shiffrin claims sixth-career overall crystal globe after finishing 11th in World Cup Finals GS - VailDaily.com
content:
Halfway through the final women’s World Cup Alpine ski race of the 2025-26 season, Emma Aicher seemed well-positioned to pull off a miracle upset. In order to claim her first overall crystal globe, the German prodigy needed a win and Mikaela Shiffrin to finish outside the top-15. After the first giant slalom run in Lillehammer, Norway, Aicher sat in third, 0.26 off Valerie Grenier’s standard while Shiffrin was 17th, 1.55 seconds off the lead. “You don’t get points in 17th here,” Shiffrin said. “So, I really could appreciate that this day could be different.” But the Edwards skier — who has steadily revived her GS form since a devastating crash in November of 2024 — came through in the clutch, posting the seventh-fastest second run to move up to 11th. In the process, Shiffrin claimed ski racing’s ultimate prize for a record-tying sixth time. “I have really this crazy birth of emotions because my team said ‘that’s it’ and I was like, ‘no it’s not,'” Shiffrin said. “We were kind of arguing about it and I was like, ‘are you really sure because I’m starting to cry and I don’t want to be actually celebrating unless it’s actually secure?'” Shiffrin matched Annemarie Moser-Pröll, who won five straight titles from 1971-75 then a sixth in 1979. The 31-year-old American won three straight from 2017-19, then back-to-back titles in 2022 and ’23. Marcel Hirscher has the all-time mark with eight overall globes. Canada’s Grenier held on for her third World Cup win on Wednesday, posting a two-run time of 2 minutes, 16.790 seconds. Norwegian Mina Fuerst Holtmann came in second and Austria’s Julia Scheib rounded out the podium. Scheib also picked up the discipline title, where Shiffrin finished fourth and fellow American, Paula Moltzan, was sixth. “It feels very special. It was a dream of mine for many years,” said Scheib, who came into the season never having stepped on a World Cup podium in the first seven years of her career. “To have it now finally in my hands feels a bit surreal but also a great moment for me.” Shiffrin started to click on the lower third of the Olympia-loypa slope. She posted the sixth-best third sector, moving up six spots and mathematically clinching the crown. “It was a lot of effort to push on the second and I was really happy with my second run, much better timing,” Shiffrin said. “I felt much more fluid and just like kind of relaxed, even though I was actually quite stressed.” While Aicher knew she couldn’t overtake her rival when she kicked out of the start for the second time, she was skiing fast through the first checkpoint. Coming off a jump into a right-hand turn, however, the 22-year-old all-around skier slid her boards sideways on the spring slush. She barely made it to the next gate, scrubbing speed which ultimately cost her nine spots. After coming into the final two races trailing Shiffrin by just 45 points, Aicher wound up 87 points back by the end. “I have to say to Emma that her skiing has been just outstanding, and today, it was just so cool to watch her, especially on the first run,” Shiffrin commented. “I really thought the outcome of this day is that she could do this. And that’s the coolest thing about ski racing, that anything is possible.” Aicher concluded her season ranked second in the overall and downhill, third in super-G, and sixth in slalom. She tallied five wins and eight podiums and also picked up a pair of silver medals at the Olympics in February. “It’s been a really fun one, also just to keep the fight going until the very last race, it’s pretty unbelievable,” Aicher told FIS. “It’s been so much fun racing and just skiing.” “I am extremely impressed by Emma’s performance this season, combining speed and slalom disciplines is very, very hard which is why almost no one is doing it anymore,” double Alpine Combined Olympic champion Michelle Gisin told FIS. “I think she just needs to keep doing what she is doing right now — it’s just perfect.” Nina O’Brien was the top American in ninth. A.J. Hurt placed 18th and Moltzan — who had the second-fastest second run by one hundredth of a second — finished in 25th. On Tuesday, Shiffrin snagged her ninth slalom win of the season en route to her ninth slalom globe — both records. Her banner campaign also included a third-career Olympic gold medal in Cortina last month. Buoyed by the tech successes of Shiffrin and early-season downhill dominance from Lindsey Vonn, the U.S. women’s team captured the FIS Alpine Nation’s Cup title for the first time since 1982. American women totaled 11 victories and 27 podium finishes as 16 different athletes contributed points across the season. “This achievement is a testament to the strength of this team from top to bottom,” U.S. Ski & Snowboard Chief of Sport Anouk Patty said in a press release on Wednesday. “To have 16 athletes scoring points and 27 podiums shows the depth and unity of this group. Everyone played a role in this success.” The 1982 team included Tamara McKinney, Cindy Nelson and Christin Cooper and was coached by John McMurtry, who spoke of those successes in a 2022 Vail Daily series, “Inside the skiing pipeline.” Are you an adventurous spirit with a love for the mountain lifestyle? title: NBA Power Rankings: The Thunder reign while East teams rise - ESPN
content:
Mike Greenberg and the "Get Up" crew discuss Victor Wembanyama's comments on the MVP race. (1:51) Where do all 30 teams stand in the final March edition of ESPN's NBA Power Rankings? So far, 10 teams have clinched at least a postseason berth, with the full playoff picture beginning to take shape. And with the pool of top 20 teams nearly set, the next few weeks are all about jockeying for position. In the Western Conference, the Los Angeles Lakers are making the loudest statement among the logjam of teams behind the Oklahoma City Thunder and San Antonio Spurs. The 3-seeded Lakers, healthy again and settling into an offensive hierarchy between Luka Doncic, LeBron James and Austin Reaves, have won nine of 10 games in their pursuit of home court in the first round. The Atlanta Hawks have made their own push as an attempt to break free from the Eastern Conference play-in picture. Atlanta is 13-1 over the past month, albeit during a weaker portion of its schedule. Things get tougher starting Wednesday, when the 6-seeded Hawks face the East-leading Detroit Pistons and Boston Celtics three times in six days. title: Gile sweeps GS at Highlands in NorAms finals - Aspen Daily News
content: Aspen native Bridger Gile charges down the hill in the second giant slalom of the NorAm Cup finals at Aspen Highlands on Tuesday. Gile, who became the youngest person to hike and ski the Highland Bowl as a 4-year-old, won both GS races on the 22nd anniversary to the week of the accomplishment. Aspen Valley Ski and Snowboard Club alumnus Tanner Perkins takes on the giant slalom on Tuesday at Aspen Highlands. County looks to update code, scrap outdated guidelines Construction projects on Aspen campus to begin in summer 202… Aspen native Bridger Gile charges down the hill in the second giant slalom of the NorAm Cup finals at Aspen Highlands on Tuesday. Gile, who became the youngest person to hike and ski the Highland Bowl as a 4-year-old, won both GS races on the 22nd anniversary to the week of the accomplishment. Twenty-two years to the week after Bridger Gile became the youngest person to hike and ski the Highland Bowl, it served as the backdrop for maybe his biggest week in pro skiing yet. With the Aspen Valley Ski and Snowboard Club hosting the North-American Cup FIS final competition of the season on Aspen Highlands, the hometown kid won not one but two giant slalom races, one each on Monday and Tuesday, securing a spot in the World Cup next season. The victories mark Gile’s first overall since March 2024, and first in the NorAms since Dec. 12, 2023. They’re his eighth and ninth overall in FIS-scoring races. “It’s probably the best way you could possibly end a season,” Gile said. “That’s really nice to go into the summer with that good, extra motivation.” Gile was dominant on Monday, finishing his two runs 1.88 seconds ahead of second-place Johs Braathen Herland of Norway and a full 2.09 seconds ahead of bronze winner Patrick Kenney, Gile’s U.S. Ski Team teammate. He finished the first run down the Golden Horn and Thunderbowl runs in 1 minute and 5.17 seconds, .35 ahead of the second-place finisher, then posted the fifth-fastest time in his second run. On a track generously treated with salt and being desperately preserved in near-summer temperatures, Gile said he was able to use his familiarity with the course to his advantage earlier on in the race. “Skiing that hill so much, just the feeling of being at home, because all season we’re racing in Europe, so to actually be able to race at home, very few people get to do that from the U.S. So that was huge, very helpful,” Gile said. “I think it’s a pretty cool connection as well, kind of growing up on this mountain and then being able to race and really, hopefully, have it be a big step for my career, is awesome.” Tuesday was closer, with the 26-year-old still .44 seconds ahead of second-place Kenney and 1.24 seconds ahead of Isaiah Nelson in third. Gile posted the second-quickest first run (1:03.75, .15 seconds behind Kenney) and the fifth-quickest run (1:04.41, 1.23 seconds behind the run winner Jack Schrady). Critically, the wins lifted him into a tie for second place in the GS discipline on the season with Nelson, trailing only Herland. He said that the two top spots from a continental cup from that continent (i.e. Americans or Canadians in the NorAm Cup) earn automatic spots in World Cup races the next season. Aspen Valley Ski and Snowboard Club alumnus Tanner Perkins takes on the giant slalom on Tuesday at Aspen Highlands. Gile got close to victories throughout the season, but took until the final competitions to get across the line. He took three straight silvers in mid-February, two at Whiteface Mountain in NorAms, then at Berchtesgaden, Germany, in the European Cup on Feb. 14. He also took two more top-10s in the GS in February before finishing 27th in the World Cup at Kranjska Gora, Slovenia, three spots off his best WC finish ever. After closing last season with a second place in the national championships, Gile is finishing a season on a high for the second straight year. But this season, he feels like that success has been more sustained late in the campaign. “Ever since, like, the end of January, beginning of February, I feel like I’ve been skiing really fast and getting better at racing, learning how to race fast, and getting consistent with it,” Gile said. “I think I was way more consistent this year, which is what it takes to make that next step. I think there’s a lot of confidence to be taken there, and I think I really improved my race craft, which is a big step from last year, as well. So, I think I’ve gotten a lot better, and the skiing itself, I think, has gotten better, and I think we’ll continue to get better every year.” The warm weather meant that, for the first time in his life, Gile went from the ski hill to the golf course after the super-G earlier in the week. He had plans to do so again on Tuesday, but had to cancel after having to attend the awards ceremony. Gile added that the course workers did the best they could given the conditions. “[The course] was amazing for how hot it was. The snow was so good,” Gile said. “The snow is going away, there’s dirt patches forming, but they saved some snow so they’re able to fill it each day. It’s been impressive what they’ve been able to pull off.” Tuesday marked the seventh day in the last eight that the NorAm finals took on Highlands (last Wednesday’s downhill training was canceled). In hot conditions, AVSC, the U.S. Ski Team and facilities workers worked to keep the course usable, amid the ice and dirt patches — including one in the finish area of the course, marked by a cone. “Despite some incredibly challenging conditions this week, I couldn’t be more proud of how our team and partners have come together to showcase what makes Aspen special,” AVSC Executive Director August Teague said via text on Tuesday. “With immense support from Aspen Skiing Co., the dedication of our staff, and the tireless work of our Director of Facilities, Cody Oates, we’ve been able to deliver a world-class event. It’s a real testament to the collaboration and commitment across this community.” The NorAm finals continue on Wednesday with the first of two women’s giant slaloms and the first of two men’s slaloms, with the second of each event wrapping up the season on Thursday.
